items.passage:
Among the most striking abilities in the animal kingdom is the octopus's capacity to change the color and texture of its skin almost instantaneously, blending into coral, sand, or rock with a fidelity that can deceive both predators and prey. What makes this feat especially remarkable is that octopuses are, by most measures, colorblind: the photoreceptors in their eyes detect brightness but not wavelength. How an animal that apparently cannot see color can nonetheless match it so precisely has puzzled biologists for decades.

Part of the answer lies in the skin itself. An octopus's skin is studded with tiny pigment-filled organs called chromatophores, each surrounded by muscles that expand or contract to reveal or conceal the pigment. Beneath these sit reflective cells that scatter and bend light. Crucially, recent research suggests that the skin may contain light-sensitive proteins of the same kind found in the eye, raising the possibility that an octopus's skin can, in a limited sense, detect the light around it and respond without waiting for instructions from the brain.

This hypothesis fits a broader and increasingly accepted picture of octopus biology: that cognition in these animals is not centralized but distributed. Roughly two-thirds of an octopus's neurons lie not in its central brain but in its arms, each of which can taste, touch, and react with a striking degree of independence. An arm severed from the body will, for a time, continue to reach toward food. Camouflage, on this view, may be less a single decision issued from a command center than the coordinated output of many semi-autonomous systems.

The implications reach beyond marine biology. Researchers in robotics and materials science have begun to study octopus skin as a model for adaptive surfaces, and philosophers of mind have seized on the octopus as a test case for theories of consciousness that assume a single, unified locus of experience. If an animal can solve a problem as complex as real-time camouflage without centralized control, then some of our most basic intuitions about how intelligence must be organized may be parochial: true of vertebrates like us, but not of intelligence as such.

questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main point of the passage?

choices.A: Because octopuses are colorblind, their celebrated ability to camouflage has been greatly exaggerated by biologists.
choices.B: The octopus's ability to camouflage despite being colorblind is best understood in light of evidence that its cognition is distributed rather than centralized, a conclusion with implications beyond biology.
choices.C: The chromatophores in an octopus's skin are controlled entirely by reflective cells that bend and scatter light.
choices.D: Robotics researchers have successfully reproduced octopus skin in the design of adaptive surfaces.
choices.E: Philosophers of mind have shown that octopuses possess a unified locus of conscious experience.

questions.explanation: The passage poses the puzzle of color-matching by a colorblind animal, answers it by appeal to skin-level light sensitivity and distributed cognition, and closes with implications beyond biology. (B) captures this arc. (A) and (E) contradict the passage; (C) overstates one detail; (D) describes an area of study, not the main point.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly suggests that which one of the following is true of an octopus's arms?

choices.A: They contain all of the animal's photoreceptors.
choices.B: They can respond to stimuli with some degree of independence from the central brain.
choices.C: They are incapable of functioning once separated from the body.
choices.D: They contain no neurons of their own.

questions.explanation: The passage states that about two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms, which can taste, touch, and react independently, and that a severed arm keeps reaching for food. That supports (B). (C) and (D) contradict the text; (A) and (E) are unsupported.

questions.stem: According to the passage, chromatophores are

choices.A: light-sensitive proteins identical to those found in the brain.
choices.B: reflective cells that scatter and bend light beneath the skin.
choices.C: pigment-filled organs surrounded by muscles that reveal or conceal the pigment.
choices.D: neurons located primarily in the octopus's arms.
choices.E: the photoreceptors responsible for detecting brightness.

questions.explanation: The second paragraph defines chromatophores as pigment-filled organs surrounded by muscles that expand or contract to reveal or conceal the pigment (C). The other choices describe different structures mentioned in the passage.

questions.stem: The author refers to philosophers of mind in the final paragraph primarily in order to

choices.A: concede that the distributed-cognition hypothesis has been refuted.
choices.B: illustrate that the octopus challenges assumptions reaching beyond biology, including about how intelligence must be organized.
choices.C: argue that consciousness requires a single, unified locus of experience.
choices.D: criticize robotics researchers for ignoring questions of consciousness.
choices.E: demonstrate that octopuses are more intelligent than vertebrates.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph uses philosophers of mind (alongside robotics) to show the octopus's implications extend beyond marine biology to assumptions about the organization of intelligence (B). (A), (C), (D), and (E) misstate the author's purpose or position.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that the puzzle that has occupied biologists arises because

choices.A: octopuses can match colors precisely even though their eyes do not detect wavelength.
choices.B: octopuses change texture more quickly than they change color.
choices.C: octopuses lose the ability to camouflage as they age.
choices.D: octopuses' arms operate independently of one another.
choices.E: octopuses rely on reflective cells rather than pigment to produce color.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph frames the puzzle as how a colorblind animal (one whose eyes detect brightness but not wavelength) can match colors so precisely (A). The other choices are either unstated or contradict the passage.

items.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, courts in many common-law countries treated a signed contract as very nearly the final word on the parties' obligations. Under the so-called 'plain meaning' rule, if the words of a written agreement were clear on their face, a judge would not consider outside evidence, such as prior negotiations or industry custom, to interpret them. The written document was presumed to embody the whole of the bargain, and the certainty this provided was thought to be worth the occasional harsh result.

Beginning in the latter half of the century, a competing approach gained ground. Its proponents argued that words never carry meaning in a vacuum; even an apparently clear term takes its sense from the context in which the parties used it. On this view, a court that refuses to look beyond the four corners of a document may enforce a meaning that neither party actually intended. Courts influenced by this reasoning began, cautiously, to admit contextual evidence even where the contract's language seemed unambiguous.

Critics of the contextual approach warn that it purchases fairness in individual cases at too high a price. If any contract can be reopened by testimony about what the parties 'really meant,' then no written term is ever truly settled, and litigation becomes both longer and less predictable. Businesses, they argue, value the ability to rely on the written word precisely because it forecloses such disputes. A rule that lets context override text, on this account, undermines the very function that written contracts are meant to serve.

Defenders respond that the contrast is overdrawn. Admitting context, they note, does not mean that a party may contradict clear terms with self-serving assertions; judges remain free to give little weight to implausible claims. The choice, they suggest, is not between certainty and chaos but between a rule that occasionally enforces meanings no one intended and one that runs a modest risk of longer disputes in exchange for interpretations that better reflect the parties' actual agreement.

questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately states the main point of the passage?

choices.A: The plain-meaning rule has been conclusively shown to produce unjust results and should be abandoned.
choices.B: Two approaches to contract interpretation, one textual and one contextual, offer competing trade-offs between certainty and fidelity to the parties' actual intent, and the debate between them is more nuanced than critics of the contextual approach suggest.
choices.C: Businesses no longer rely on written contracts because courts admit contextual evidence.
choices.D: Courts in common-law countries have uniformly rejected the plain-meaning rule in favor of a contextual approach.
choices.E: The meaning of a contract term can always be determined without reference to outside evidence.

questions.explanation: The passage lays out the plain-meaning rule, the rise of the contextual approach, the critics' objection, and the defenders' reply that the contrast is overdrawn. (B) captures this balanced arc. (A), (C), (D), and (E) overstate or contradict the passage.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that defenders of the contextual approach would most likely agree with which one of the following?

choices.A: A party should be permitted to override a clear contract term with any assertion about intent.
choices.B: Judges are unable to distinguish plausible from implausible claims about the parties' intentions.
choices.C: Admitting contextual evidence need not make clear terms meaningless, because judges can discount unconvincing claims.
choices.D: Certainty in contract enforcement is never a legitimate goal.
choices.E: Written contracts should be abolished in favor of oral agreements.

questions.explanation: Defenders argue that admitting context 'does not mean that a party may contradict clear terms with self-serving assertions' and that judges may give little weight to implausible claims (C). (A) and (B) are the opposite of their position; (D) and (E) are far stronger than anything they assert.

questions.stem: The primary purpose of the third paragraph is to

choices.A: present the objection that the contextual approach sacrifices predictability and reliance for case-by-case fairness.
choices.B: provide statistical evidence that litigation has become more expensive.
choices.C: endorse the plain-meaning rule as the correct approach.
choices.D: describe the historical origins of written contracts.
choices.E: explain how judges weigh implausible claims about intent.

questions.explanation: The third paragraph voices the critics' warning that the contextual approach trades away certainty and reliance for individual fairness (A). It offers no statistics (B), does not itself endorse a rule (C), gives no history (D), and the weighing of implausible claims appears in the next paragraph (E).

questions.stem: According to the passage, under the plain-meaning rule a judge would

choices.A: always consider prior negotiations before interpreting a contract.
choices.B: decline to consider outside evidence when a written term is clear on its face.
choices.C: give equal weight to text and to industry custom in every case.
choices.D: reopen any contract on the basis of testimony about intent.
choices.E: treat oral agreements as superior to written ones.

questions.explanation: The first paragraph states that under the plain-meaning rule, if the words are clear, a judge 'would not consider outside evidence' such as prior negotiations or custom (B). The other choices contradict that description.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the debate between the two approaches is best described as

choices.A: dismissive of both positions as equally baseless.
choices.B: convinced that the plain-meaning rule is indefensible.
choices.C: even-handed, presenting the trade-offs of each side without strongly endorsing either.
choices.D: alarmed by the rise of the contextual approach.
choices.E: indifferent to the practical consequences for businesses.

questions.explanation: The author lays out each approach's rationale and the objections and replies without declaring a winner, characterizing the choice as one of trade-offs (C). (A), (B), (D), and (E) attribute a stronger or one-sided stance the author does not take.

items.passage:
When economists first began to study happiness, they encountered a paradox. Within a given country at a given time, wealthier people report being happier than poorer people, on average. Yet as entire nations grew richer over the decades following the Second World War, average reported happiness in those nations scarcely rose at all. If money buys happiness for individuals, why did it seem to buy so little for whole societies?

One influential explanation holds that much of the satisfaction people derive from income is relative rather than absolute. On this account, what matters is not how much one earns in isolation but how much one earns compared with others in one's community. When everyone's income rises together, relative positions stay the same, and so, the theory predicts, does happiness. A raise makes a person happier only if it lifts that person above peers whose incomes have not changed.

The relative-income hypothesis is elegant, but it does not fit all the evidence. More recent studies drawing on larger and more consistent international data have found that richer countries do tend to report higher average happiness than poorer ones, and that as a country's income grows, so, modestly, does its reported well-being. These findings suggest that absolute income matters after all, even if its effects are easy to miss in noisier early data.

Most researchers now favor a reconciling view. Both absolute and relative income appear to influence well-being, but each is subject to diminishing returns: an additional thousand dollars raises the happiness of a poor household far more than that of a rich one. Because the wealthy nations studied in the earliest research had already reached income levels where further gains yield little, those studies may simply have been examining the part of the curve where the effect of money is smallest.

questions.stem: Which one of the following most accurately expresses the main point of the passage?

choices.A: Money has no measurable effect on human happiness at either the individual or the national level.
choices.B: The relative-income hypothesis has been shown to be entirely mistaken.
choices.C: Although the relative-income hypothesis was proposed to explain why growing national wealth did not raise happiness, later evidence supports a reconciling view in which both absolute and relative income matter, subject to diminishing returns.
choices.D: Wealthier people within a country are no happier than poorer people.
choices.E: Early happiness research was based on data that were deliberately falsified.

questions.explanation: The passage introduces the paradox, presents the relative-income explanation, notes contrary evidence for absolute income, and settles on a reconciling diminishing-returns view (C). (A), (B), (D), and (E) contradict or overstate the passage.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly supports which one of the following statements about the diminishing-returns view?

choices.A: It denies that relative income has any effect on well-being.
choices.B: It implies that a given increase in income raises happiness more for a poor household than for a wealthy one.
choices.C: It holds that happiness depends solely on absolute income.
choices.D: It rejects all findings from recent international studies.
choices.E: It predicts that national happiness cannot rise under any circumstances.

questions.explanation: The reconciling view states each kind of income is subject to diminishing returns, so an extra thousand dollars helps a poor household far more than a rich one (B). (A) and (C) ignore that the view keeps both factors; (D) and (E) contradict the passage.

questions.stem: The author most likely discusses the more recent international studies in the third paragraph in order to

choices.A: provide evidence that challenges the relative-income hypothesis as a complete explanation.
choices.B: prove that relative income has no effect on happiness.
choices.C: show that early researchers acted dishonestly.
choices.D: argue that happiness cannot be measured across countries.
choices.E: conclude that wealthy nations are less happy than poor ones.

questions.explanation: The recent studies show richer countries report higher happiness and that growth modestly raises well-being, which undercuts the relative-income hypothesis as a full account (A). (B) overstates; (C), (D), and (E) misdescribe the passage.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the relative-income hypothesis predicts that a person's raise will increase that person's happiness only if

choices.A: the raise is larger than one thousand dollars.
choices.B: the raise lifts the person above peers whose incomes have not changed.
choices.C: the entire nation's income rises at the same time.
choices.D: the person was already wealthy before the raise.

questions.explanation: The second paragraph states that under the hypothesis a raise makes a person happier only if it lifts that person above peers whose incomes have not changed (B). (C) would keep relative position unchanged and thus, per the theory, not raise happiness; the others are unsupported.
